A B S T R A C T
The rapid rise of artificial intelligence (AI) and the exponential growth of AI-based tools, particularly LLMs, have
spurred extensive global research in recent years.
In the education sector, LLMs have demonstrated their po­
tential to enhance students' learning experiences and contribute to improved academic performance.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf

ing experiences and contribute to improved academic performance.
In Vietnam,
while recent studies have explored university students' perceptions of using LLMs in learning, there is a notable
research gap regarding their actual adoption of these tools for educational purposes.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: The psychology of AI adoption in education: University students' intentions

g their actual adoption of these tools for educational purposes.
Therefore, this research was
conducted to address this gap by investigating the intentions to adopt LLMs for learning by university students,
applying the extended Technology Adoption Model (TAM) and Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) models.
The
study employed a non-probability convenience sampling approach to gather data from 226 university students in
Vietnam who have prior experiences in adapting LLMs for their learning.

Partial Least Square Structural Equation
Modeling (PLS-SEM) was employed to test the hypotheses and to evaluate the research model.
The findings
revealed that perceived ease of use, perceived usefulness, and perceived trust in LLMs significantly and positively
influence students' attitudes toward adoption.

influence students' attitudes toward adoption.
Moreover, all TPB constructs-attitude, subjective norms, and
perceived behavioral control-positively impact intention to use LLMs, with subjective norms having the
strongest effect.
These findings underscore the critical role of social influence in shaping students' adoption
behavior within this cultural context.

Practically, it provides clear

Introduction
Large Language Models (LLMs), a sophisticated form of artificial
intelligence (AI), are rapidly transforming the landscape of natural
language processing (NLP).

These models are built upon transformer

datasets (Raffel et al., 2020; Tay et al., 2022; Vaswani et al., 2017), have
become fundamental tools for a wide range of NLP tasks (Floridi &

Their capabilities, including fluent question answering
and human-like text generation (Hoang et al.

2.1.
The Technology Acceptance Model (TAM)
2.1.1.
Technology Acceptance Model (TAM)
Rooted in information systems research, the Technology Acceptance
Model (TAM) provides a strong foundation for understanding technol­
ogy adoption.
Initially introduced by Davis (1985), TAM posits that
perceived usefulness (PU) and perceived ease of use (PEOU) are key
psychological determinants of technology adoption.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: Quy et al., 2023). Their multi-tasking capabilities encompass question

text:
PEOU) are key
psychological determinants of technology adoption.
According to the
model, individuals are more inclined to adopt a technology if they
believe it is easy to use and will enhance their task performance.
Davis
(1993) later expanded TAM to include user attitudes (ATT) as a medi­
ating factor influencing adoption decisions.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: Quy et al., 2023). Their multi-tasking capabilities encompass question

text:
es (ATT) as a medi­
ating factor influencing adoption decisions.
Perceived usefulness (PU), as defined by Davis (1985), reflects the
extent to which a user believes employing a particular technology will
provide benefits and value by improving their job effectiveness or
helping them achieve their objectives.
This belief in the technology's
capacity to provide tangible benefits is a key motivator for adoption.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: Quy et al., 2023). Their multi-tasking capabilities encompass question

text:
ty to provide tangible benefits is a key motivator for adoption.
Perceived Ease of Use (PEOU), on the other hand, reflects a user's
perception of the effort required to interact with a system, essentially
referring to an individual's belief about how effortless or easy it will be
to operate a specific system (Davis, 1985).
A system perceived as easier
to use is more likely to be seen as useful, as users can readily apply it to
their work without significant mental or physical effort.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: Quy et al., 2023). Their multi-tasking capabilities encompass question

text:
it to
their work without significant mental or physical effort.
Within the TAM framework, both PEOU and PU influence the user's
attitude toward using the technology.
This attitude, whether positive or
negative, subsequently affects the user's behavioral intention to use the
technology (Davis, 1985).
This attitude acts as a mediator between the
fundamental beliefs about the technology (PU and PEOU) and their
subsequent intention to use it (Davis, 1993).

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf

nd PEOU) and their
subsequent intention to use it (Davis, 1993).
Positive perceptions of a
technology's usefulness and ease of use typically cultivate a more
favorable attitude toward its adoption.
Adoption intention (INT) is
defined as an individual's subjective likelihood of engaging in a specific
behavior (Fishbein & Ajzen, 1975).

ood of engaging in a specific
behavior (Fishbein & Ajzen, 1975).
In the context of technology adop­
tion, research has consistently demonstrated a strong link between
students' intentions to adopt technology-based learning tools and their
actual usage behavior (Al-Qirim et al., 2017).

2.1.2.
Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)
Developed by Ajzen (1991) and rooted in his earlier Theory of
Reasoned Action (TRA) (Ajzen, 1985), the Theory of Planned Behavior
(TPB) provides a framework for understanding human behavior,
particularly in situations where individuals lack complete volitional
control.
The Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) suggests that behavioral
intentions can be effectively predicted by considering attitudes toward
the behavior, subjective norms, and perceived behavioral control.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: 2.1.2. Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)

text:
he behavior, subjective norms, and perceived behavioral control.
These
three factors collectively influence the formation of behavioral in­
tentions, which in turn drive subsequent actions (Bosnjak et al., 2020).
An attitude toward a behavior is "an individual's positive or negative
feelings (evaluative affect) about performing the target behavior"
(Fishbein & Ajzen, 1975, p.
216).

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: 2.1.2. Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)

text:
erforming the target behavior"
(Fishbein & Ajzen, 1975, p.
216).
A subjective norm is "the person's
perception that most people who are important to him think he should
or should not perform the behavior in question" (Fishbein & Ajzen,
1975, p.
302).
Subjective norms influence intention by shaping in­
dividuals' decisions based on social approval.
Even if they do not
personally favor a behavior, they may engage in it if significant others
approve and they feel pressured to conform.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: 2.1.2. Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)

text:
f significant others
approve and they feel pressured to conform.
Crucially, TPB also includes
perceived behavioral control (PBC) (Antonietti et al., 2022), which ac­
counts for the perceived ability to perform a behavior.
The concept of
perceived behavioral control refers to the belief that one has control
over his or her actions.
Assuming sufficient actual control exists, in­
dividuals are expected to act on their intentions when the opportunity
arises (Ajzen, 2002).

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: 2.1.2. Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)

text:
t on their intentions when the opportunity
arises (Ajzen, 2002).
This inclusion of PBC makes TPB particularly
relevant for understanding the complexities of technology adoption,
especially in situations where individuals may experience barriers or
have limited complete control over technology use (Yuen et al., 2020).

2.2.1.
Impact of TAM components on attitude
The Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) has proven to be a valu­
able framework for understanding the adoption of educational tech­
nologies, including the burgeoning field of AI-driven tools like Large
Language Models (LLMs).
Numerous recent studies have investigated
TAM's applicability and explanatory power across diverse educational
settings and learner demographics in the context of AI.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: 2.1.2. Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB)

cational
settings and learner demographics in the context of AI.
For instance,
research by Lin (2024) and Malik et al.
(2021), focusing on Chinese
university students, confirmed that both perceived usefulness (PU) and
perceived ease of use (PEOU) significantly predicted attitudes and
behavioral intentions toward using AI chatbots.
These findings align
with broader applications of TAM in AI contexts, suggesting its
continued relevance when users have some familiarity with AI tools.

(2024) investigated
factors influencing AI education engagement among K-12 students in
China.
Their augmented model incorporated cognitive factors like AI
intrinsic motivation, readiness, confidence, and anxiety, alongside
human-computer interaction elements within generative AI (GenAI) tool
usage.

among younger, less experienced users.
This highlights the potential for
enriching TAM with motivational and affective dimensions when
examining emerging educational technologies that require not only us­
ability but also cognitive engagement.

Their study confirmed

This work is particularly valuable as it reinforces the cross-

However, not all studies have uniformly supported TAM's traditional

Impact of perceived trust on attitude
While these models effectively present technology adoption, their
application to AI-driven educational tools, such as LLMs, necessitates
consideration of additional factors, including ethical and pedagogical
concerns.
Issues surrounding academic integrity, misinformation, and
over-reliance on AI-generated content have been widely debated (Rasul
et al., 2023).

4.1.
Measurement model assessment
The first part of the analysis involved computing the Cronbach's
alpha, outer loadings, composite reliability (CR), and average variance
extracted (AVE) for all constructs to assess their reliability and conver­
gent validity.
For a construct to be considered reliable, its observed
variables must have outer loading factors of at least 0.5 and a composite
reliability (CR) of at least or greater than 0.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: distinct from the others, and they consistently measured their intended

text:
and a composite
reliability (CR) of at least or greater than 0.7 (Hulland, 1999).
The uti­
lization of Cronbach's alpha is to eliminate unnecessary variables, with a
threshold of more than 0.6 to ensure scale reliability (Nunnally &
Bernstein, 1994).
Convergent validity, used to assess scale stability, is

influence on students' attitudes toward LLM-based technologies.

Implications
6.1.
Theoretical implications
From a theoretical standpoint, this study makes several notable
contributions to the existing body of knowledge on technology adoption
and the integration of AI in educational contexts.
Firstly, it provides
further empirical validation for the applicability of the Technology
Acceptance Model (TAM) in explaining university students' initial atti­
tudes toward and intentions to use Large Language Model (LLM)-based
technologies for learning.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: 6. Implications

text:
use Large Language Model (LLM)-based
technologies for learning.
The confirmation of the significant direct in­
fluence of perceived usefulness and perceived ease of use on students'
attitudes, which subsequently drive their intention to use, reinforces the
robustness of TAM in predicting technology acceptance within emerging
AI-powered educational technologies.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: 6. Implications

text:
acceptance within emerging
AI-powered educational technologies.
Secondly, this research extends the application of the Theory of
Planned Behavior (TPB) to the specific context of LLM adoption in
higher education.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf

text:
PB) to the specific context of LLM adoption in
higher education.
The findings support the significant positive influence
of attitude toward the behavior, subjective norm, and perceived
behavioral control on students' intention to use LLMs, underscoring the
relevance of considering not only individual beliefs but also social in­
fluences and perceived capabilities in understanding adoption behaviors
related to AI-driven learning tools.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: 6. Implications

text:
standing adoption behaviors
related to AI-driven learning tools.
Notably, the finding that subjective
norm emerged as the strongest predictor of intention highlights the
critical role of the social environment and the opinions of significant
referents in shaping students' decisions regarding LLM integration into
their learning practices.
Thirdly, the inclusion of perceived trust as an antecedent to attitude
offers a valuable extension to traditional technology adoption models in
the context of AI.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf
metadata.section_heading: 6. Implications

to traditional technology adoption models in
the context of AI.
The significant positive relationship observed between
perceived trust in LLMs and students' attitudes suggests that trust is a
crucial psychological factor influencing the initial acceptance of these
AI-based learning tools.
This finding contributes to the growing body of
literature examining the role of trust in human-AI interaction within
educational settings.

perceived ease of use and attitude toward LLMs may vary across

6.2.
Practical implications
The findings of this study offer actionable insights for educators,
university administrators, developers of AI-based educational technol­
ogies, and policymakers seeking to effectively integrate Large Language
Models (LLMs) into higher education in Vietnam.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf

text:
e Large Language
Models (LLMs) into higher education in Vietnam.
First, the established significance of perceived usefulness and
perceived ease of use in shaping students' attitudes necessitates a clear
articulation of the academic advantages offered by LLMs, coupled with
an emphasis on their intuitive design.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf

red by LLMs, coupled with
an emphasis on their intuitive design.
To cultivate favorable attitudes
and subsequent adoption intentions, it is crucial to demonstrate how
these tools can demonstrably improve learning outcomes while mini­
mizing the perceived cognitive burden associated with their utilization.

metadata.source_file: The psychology of AI adoption in education.pdf

he perceived cognitive burden associated with their utilization.
Universities should organize discipline-specific workshops and tutorials
that showcase how LLMs can support academic tasks such as drafting
research outlines in social sciences, solving coding problems in engi­
neering, or refining language in humanities assignments.
These use cases
must be aligned with curriculum outcomes to highlight how LLMs
directly enhance academic performance.

directly enhance academic performance.
In parallel, to address issues
related to perceived ease of use, developers should involve students in
participatory usability testing to identify common barriers and frustra­
tions.
Using this feedback, platform designers can enhance the user
interface by simplifying navigation, minimizing technical complexity,
and embedding contextual guidance tools-such as tooltips, step-by-
step prompts, and real-time suggestions-into the system.

Second, the strong influence of subjective norms highlights the
critical role of both instructors and peer groups in driving students'
adoption of LLMs.

structors and peer groups in driving students'

Educators who demonstrate a positive attitude to­
ward LLMs and intentionally incorporate them into their teaching
practices can significantly shape student perceptions and encourage
responsible use.
Academic leaders should formalize this approach by
integrating LLM-supported activities into course designs and assess­
ments, thereby signaling institutional support.

Simultaneously, fostering
a classroom culture where LLM usage is openly discussed, shared, and
normalized among peers can further reinforce its acceptance as a valu­
able learning tool.
Third, the strong influence of perceived trust highlights the critical
responsibility of both LLM developers and educational institutions in
ensuring the credibility and transparency of AI-generated content.

To
build and sustain student confidence, developers should prioritize the
accuracy, neutrality, and verifiability of outputs by incorporating fea­

indicators of uncertainty or potential bias.
These elements not only
enhance reliability but also help users critically evaluate the information

Institutions can further reinforce trust by issuing
clear ethical guidelines on LLM use, specifying appropriate applications
(e.g., research assistance, drafting support) and boundaries (e.g.,

Providing students with practical training

Lastly, the strong effect of perceived behavioral control on adoption
intention emphasizes the need to empower students with the confidence
and skills to effectively use LLMs.

Educational institutions should
implement structured training programs, such as workshops, orientation
modules, or digital literacy courses, that provide step-by-step guidance
on essential LLM competencies, including crafting effective prompts,
critically evaluating outputs, and ethically incorporating AI-generated
content into academic work.

To provide continuous support beyond
initial training, universities should also establish dedicated resources
such as academic help desks, peer mentoring services, or online support
platforms to assist students in navigating challenges related to LLM use.
These initiatives will not only strengthen students' self-efficacy but also
promote sustained, responsible engagement with AI tools.

By implementing these targeted interventions-focused on real-

communication, and structured support-educational institutions can
move beyond awareness-raising and foster sustainable, responsible
adoption of LLMs in Vietnamese higher education.
These practical steps
will contribute to more empowered, self-directed learners and a more
AI-integrated learning environment.

7.
Limitations and recommendations
This research comes with certain limitations.
First, it is essential to
acknowledge the constraints posed by the sample dataset, which com­
prises only 226 respondents, predominantly university students in the
Southern region of Vietnam.
As a result, the applicability of the findings
to a larger student population is restricted.

to a larger student population is restricted.
Second, the survey subjects
of this study are primarily students, which has not adequately covered
the qualifications and the respondents' age.
Thus, it potentially affects
the equality of the results, which may only be shown in one direction.
This research implies that university students are likely to

Conclusion
The primary objective of this research is to investigate university
students' adoption intentions regarding Large Language Models (LLMs)
as tools to support their learning.

Large Language Models (LLMs)
as tools to support their learning.
The research employed an integrated
model that combines the extended Technology Acceptance Model
(TAM) and the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) as its conceptual
framework.
The study's results validated both models within this
context.

.
The study's results validated both models within this
context.
Specifically, the findings demonstrated that all elements of
extended TAM, including perceived ease of use (PEOU), perceived use­
fulness (PU), and perceived trust (PT) have a positive influence on stu­
dents' attitudes (ATT) toward using LLMs for their learning, explaining
44.1 % of the variance.

sing LLMs for their learning, explaining
44.1 % of the variance.
Accordingly, students are more likely to hold a
favorable disposition toward the implementation of LLMs if they
perceive the technology to be beneficial, user-friendly, and reliable.

Additionally, the TPB was validated, as the results show that perceived
behavioral control (PBC), subjective norms (SN), and attitude (ATT) are
all significant predictors of adoption intention (INT) for LLMs in the
context of learning, with the proposed model explaining 53.7 % of INT.

These findings suggest that students are more likely to adopt LLM
technology if they and their important social referents perceive LLMs
positively and if they have a sense of control over how LLMs are used.

mote the use of advanced technologies like LLMs for university students.
It is crucial to address ethical issues such as plagiarism and overreliance
on these tools.
